title: Greedy & Intervals
summary: Flipkart-style OAs lean *hard* on greedy, and it's your fastest topic to sharpen: you already have CP instinct for sorting and local choices. This module locks in the three interval templates (merge, schedule, reach) and — the part that actually wins interviews — the one-sentence **exchange argument** that tells you a greedy is correct *before* you ship it.

notes.heading: How to smell a greedy problem
notes.body:
Greedy problems rarely announce themselves — you have to recognize the tells:

- The verb is **maximize / minimize / fewest / most**, and a locally best choice (earliest finish, largest bill, cheapest now) plausibly stacks up into a global optimum.
- The input is — or can be — **sorted**, then handled in one pass: "schedule these", "select the most", "minimum number of X to cover Y".
- **Intervals** appear: `[start, end]` pairs, meetings, merges, overlaps, coverage.
- Every decision is **irreversible**: you commit, move forward, and never need to undo.

You already have the CP instinct for local choices and sorting. What this module adds is the *interval* pattern vocabulary, and — more importantly — the discipline to tell a correct greedy from a plausible-but-wrong one when the clock is running.

> [!WARNING] The "it's actually DP" alarm
> If the best choice *now* depends on choices you haven't made yet, or there are **two resource dimensions** at once (weight *and* value — that's 0/1 knapsack), greedy is a trap. Spend 30 seconds hunting for a small counterexample. If you find even one, switch to DP without regret.

> [!KEY]
> A greedy solution is three things: the **sort key**, the **one value you carry** across the scan, and a **one-sentence proof**. If you can't name all three, you don't have a greedy yet — you have a hope.

notes.heading: The exchange argument — your one-sentence proof
notes.body:
Here is the tool that separates a defensible greedy from a guess. To show greedy = optimal, you argue: *take any optimal solution; if its choice differs from mine, I can **swap** my greedy choice in without making it worse.* Repeat the swap step by step and the greedy is at least as good as the optimal — so it **is** optimal.

> [!ANALOGY]
> Think of booking back-to-back sessions in a shared room. If someone claims a "better" schedule that doesn't start with the earliest-finishing booking, hand them yours instead: it frees the room *sooner*, so everything in their schedule still fits. Their plan was never better — at best it tied.

In an interview you don't write this out as a formal induction. You **say the swap in one sentence**:

> [!KEY] The sentence to say out loud
> "If the optimal solution didn't pick the earliest-finishing interval, I can replace its first pick with mine and free up at least as much room — so my pick is never worse."

That single sentence is what makes an interviewer trust your greedy. It also protects *you*: if you try to state the swap and can't — if swapping your choice in could genuinely hurt later options — that's your signal the problem isn't greedy at all.

> [!EXAM]
> In a timed OA nobody reads your proof, but the exchange argument is still your *correctness check*. Before coding, spend 20 seconds trying to break your own greedy with a 3-element counterexample. Wrong-greedy submissions are the most expensive mistake in a 90-minute, 3-hard format.

notes.heading: Template 1 — Merge intervals: sort by START, extend the end
Template 2 — Interval scheduling: sort by END
Template 3 — Reachability greedy: carry the farthest
Pitfalls, boundaries, and how to narrate it
notes.body:
The bugs in this module are all *boundary* bugs — the algorithms are short, so precision is everything.

| Bug | Fix |
|---|---|
| Wrong sort key | Merge → **start**; max non-overlapping / arrows → **end**. #1 error in this topic. |
| `>` vs `>=` on touching endpoints | `[1,2]` and `[2,3]`: for scheduling they're usually compatible (`>=` keeps both); for arrows touching **does** overlap (use strict `>`). Read the problem's examples — never assume. |
| Bad init | `lastEnd = INT_MIN` (or `LLONG_MIN` for large coordinates) so the first interval is always taken. |
| Jump Game II off-by-one | Loop `i + 1 < nums.size()` — never count a jump *from* the last index. |
| Empty input | Return the sane base case (`0` rooms, empty list) before touching `res.back()`. |

> [!PITFALL] The greedy-vs-DP litmus test
> Coin change with coins `{1,3,4}` making 6: largest-first greedy gives `4+1+1` = 3 coins, but `3+3` = 2 is optimal. 0/1 knapsack by value/weight ratio fails the same way. If a 30-second counterexample exists, it *will* be in the hidden tests — abandon greedy and go DP.

> [!EXAM] Interview narration, every single time
> 1. Restate the pattern: "this is interval scheduling / a reachability greedy."
> 2. Name the sort key and the carried value: "sort by end, track the last chosen end."
> 3. Say the one-sentence exchange argument.
> 4. Mention the $O(n \log n)$ sort cost, then code.
> 5. If unsure, say "let me sanity-check with a counterexample" and try `[0,10],[1,2],[3,4]` out loud — that's a strong signal, not a weak one.

problems.name: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock
problems.note: One pass: track the running minimum price, greedily update best profit.

problems.name: Assign Cookies
problems.note: Sort both arrays, two-pointer greedy matching — the textbook exchange argument.

problems.name: Lemonade Change
problems.note: Always give change with the largest bills first; just count your 5s and 10s.

problems.name: Jump Game
problems.note: Carry one value — `farthest` reachable; if `i` ever passes it, you're stuck.

problems.name: Jump Game II
problems.note: Minimum jumps = BFS-like level greedy; bump `jumps` when `i` hits `curEnd`.

problems.name: Merge Intervals
problems.note: Sort by **start**, then either extend `res.back()[1]` with `max` or push new.

problems.name: Insert Interval
problems.note: Three-phase scan: copy the befores, merge everything overlapping, copy the afters.

problems.name: Non-overlapping Intervals
problems.note: Interval scheduling: sort by **end**, count keepers; answer = n − kept.

problems.name: Minimum Number of Arrows to Burst Balloons
problems.note: Sort by end, shoot at each earliest end — touching counts as overlap, so use strict `>`.

problems.name: Meeting Rooms II
problems.note: **Premium** — max concurrent intervals via min-heap of ends or a +1/−1 sweep; free on LintCode 919 / NeetCode.

problems.name: Partition Labels
problems.note: Precompute each char's last occurrence; greedily extend the window to the max last-index seen.

problems.name: Gas Station
problems.note: Single pass: running `tank` picks the start, running `total` decides feasibility.

problems.name: Task Scheduler
problems.note: Greedy on the most-frequent task; idle-slot formula `(maxFreq−1)·(n+1)+countOfMax` vs just `tasks`.

problems.name: Candy
problems.note: Two greedy passes: left→right handles rising neighbors, right→left handles falling.

deepdives.q: **Interval scheduling: why END, not start** — given intervals, keep the maximum number that don't overlap (equivalently: remove the fewest, or shoot the fewest arrows to burst every balloon). Which sort key makes the greedy correct, and how do you prove it?
deepdives.answer:
**Why the tempting greedy fails.** "Sort by start, keep picking" dies on `[0,10], [1,2], [3,4]`: sorting by start picks `[0,10]` first, it hogs the whole line, and you keep 1 interval when you could keep 2.

[[fig:m14-sort-by-end]]

The other tempting fix, "sort by shortest length," also fails: on chains like `[1,3],[2,4],[3,5]`, a length-based pick can conflict with *two* neighbors at once. Length simply isn't the invariant that matters.

> [!INTUITION] The trick
> Sort by **END time** and always take the interval that finishes earliest among those still compatible. Finishing earliest **leaves the most room** for everything after it — that's the exchange argument made physical.

```cpp
sort(iv.begin(), iv.end(), [](auto&a, auto&b){ return a[1] < b[1]; });
int kept = 0, lastEnd = INT_MIN;
for (auto& x : iv) {
    if (x[0] >= lastEnd) { kept++; lastEnd = x[1]; } // compatible -> take it
}
// Non-overlapping Intervals answer = n - kept  (min removals)
```

**The exchange-argument proof (say this out loud).** Let the greedy's first pick be *g*, the globally earliest end. Take any optimal solution; its first pick *o* ends no earlier than *g*. Swap *o* out for *g*: the solution stays valid — *g* ends earlier, so it can't conflict with anything *o* didn't already conflict with — and the count is unchanged. Induct on the rest, and the greedy is optimal.

**Arrows is the identical problem in costume.** "Burst all balloons with the fewest arrows" = "how few points hit all intervals". Sort by end; shoot at each interval's end; skip every balloon that arrow already covers. One boundary flip: for arrows, *touching endpoints count as overlapping*, so the compatibility test uses strict `>` instead of `>=`.

**Complexity:** $O(n \log n)$ for the sort plus an $O(n)$ scan; $O(1)$ extra space (or $O(\log n)$ for the sort).

**What generalizes:** any "select the most non-conflicting things" question — meetings, courses, ads — is this template. Your only real decisions are the sort key and the boundary comparison.

deepdives.q: **Meeting Rooms II** — given meeting time intervals, find the *minimum number of conference rooms* required (i.e., the maximum number of meetings alive at any instant).
**Jump Game II** — `nums[i]` is the maximum jump length from index `i`. Return the *minimum number of jumps* to reach the last index, in better than $O(n^2)$.

**Gas Station** — a circular route with `gas[i]` available at station `i` and `cost[i]` to reach the next station. Return the unique starting index from which you can complete a full loop, or `-1`, in one pass.

quiz.q: An OA asks: *"Given `n` meetings, attend the maximum possible number (one at a time)."* What do you sort by, and what single value do you carry across the scan?
options: Sort by start time; track the earliest start seen so far
Sort by end time; track the end of the last meeting you committed to
Sort by duration (shortest first); track total time used
Sort by start time; track a min-heap of end times
quiz.explanation: This is interval scheduling: earliest finish leaves the most room, so sort by **end** and keep `lastEnd`. Option D is the *Meeting Rooms II* setup — a heap of ends counts max *concurrent* meetings (rooms), which is a different question. Sorting by start or duration both have easy 3-interval counterexamples.

quiz.q: In the `maxNonOverlap` template you change `if (x[0] >= lastEnd)` to `if (x[0] > lastEnd)`. What actually changes?
options: Nothing — the two are equivalent because the intervals are sorted
The first interval is never taken, so the count is always off by one
Intervals that merely *touch* (one ends exactly where the next starts) now count as overlapping, so you may keep fewer intervals
The loop can read past the end of the array
quiz.explanation: With `>=`, `[1,2]` and `[2,3]` are compatible; with `>`, a start equal to `lastEnd` is rejected. The first interval is still taken either way (any start beats `INT_MIN`). This boundary flip is exactly the scheduling-vs-arrows distinction — for arrows, touching *does* overlap, so there you *want* the strict `>`.

quiz.q: For the min-heap version of Meeting Rooms II on `n` meetings, what are the time and extra-space complexities?
options: $O(n \log n)$ time, $O(n)$ space
$O(n)$ time, $O(1)$ space
$O(n^2)$ time, $O(n)$ space
$O(n \log n)$ time, $O(1)$ space
quiz.explanation: Sorting costs $O(n \log n)$, and each meeting does at most one push and one pop at $O(\log n)$ each. The heap can hold up to $n$ end times when everything overlaps, so extra space is $O(n)$ — which rules out option D. There's no $O(n)$-time solution because sorting is required.

quiz.q: Trace the Jump Game II greedy on `nums = [2,3,1,1,4]`. What does it return?
quiz.explanation: `i=0`: farthest = 2, `i == curEnd(0)` → jumps = 1, curEnd = 2. `i=1`: farthest = max(2, 1+3) = 4. `i=2`: `i == curEnd` → jumps = 2, curEnd = 4. The loop stops before the last index (the `i + 1 < n` guard), so the answer is **2**: jump 0→1, then 1→4. If you'd looped through the last index, you'd have wrongly counted a third jump.

quiz.q: Coins `{1,3,4}`, target 6. The largest-coin-first greedy produces `4+1+1` (3 coins). What's the correct takeaway?
options: The greedy is correct — 3 coins is optimal for 6
The greedy fails only because the coins weren't sorted ascending
Running the greedy from the smallest coin instead fixes it
The greedy is wrong (`3+3` uses 2 coins) — coin change with arbitrary denominations needs DP
quiz.explanation: `3+3 = 6` with 2 coins beats the greedy's 3. The best choice *now* (take a 4) depends on what remains makeable *later* — the tell that this is DP territory. Sorting direction doesn't rescue it in either direction. Greedy on coins only works for special systems like `{1,5,10,25}`; hidden tests will find the counterexample if you gamble.

quiz.q: In Gas Station, your tank goes negative arriving at station `i` after starting at `s`. Why may the next candidate start jump straight to `i+1`?
options: Every station in `s..i` was reached with tank ≥ 0, so starting there means arriving at `i` with even less fuel — they all fail too
Because the tank going negative proves total gas < total cost, so the answer is -1
Because the answer is always the station with the maximum `gas[i] − cost[i]`
It may not — each start in `s..i` must be tested separately, which is why the algorithm is O(n²)
quiz.explanation: That's Lemma 2: starting at any intermediate station `k` only *removes* the non-negative cushion you had when you passed `k`, so the trip fails at `i` again. This disqualifies the whole prefix at once, making one pass suffice — the algorithm is $O(n)$, not $O(n^2)$. A negative tank says nothing about the *total*; that's a separate check at the end.

quiz.q: You run the merge-intervals template but **forget to sort** first, on `[[1,4],[0,2],[3,5]]`. What does it return?
quiz.explanation: Take `[1,4]`. Then `[0,2]`: `0 > 4` is false → "overlap", end = max(4,2) = 4, still `[1,4]`. Then `[3,5]`: `3 > 4` is false → end = max(4,5) = 5, giving `[[1,5]]` — which silently loses coverage of `[0,1)`. The correct answer after sorting is `[[0,5]]`. The template's whole invariant — overlaps only ever touch `res.back()` — exists *because* of the sort.

m14-merge-timeline.caption: Merging `[[1,4],[3,6],[8,10]]`: sorted by start, `[3,6]` overlaps the growing interval so its end extends to 6; `[8,10]` starts past 6 so it begins a new one.

m14-sort-by-end.caption: The counterexample that kills sort-by-start: `[0,10]` picked first blocks both small intervals (kept 1). Sorting by **end** takes `[1,2]` then `[3,4]` and skips `[0,10]` (kept 2).
m14-sort-by-end.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 660 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Counterexample showing why interval scheduling sorts by end time">
  <text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">why sort by END: intervals [0,10] [1,2] [3,4]</text>
  <text x="20" y="52" class="t-sm t-bad">sort by start: pick [0,10] first — it hogs the whole line</text>

m14-jump-levels.caption: Jump Game II on `[2,3,1,1,4]` seen as BFS levels: level 1 is everything index 0 reaches (`curEnd = 2`), level 2 is everything level 1 reaches (index 4). Two levels = two jumps, no queue needed.

m14-sweep-line.caption: Meeting Rooms II as a sweep line: each meeting contributes `+1` at its start and `−1` at its end; the running count below the axis peaks at **2**, so 2 rooms suffice.

  <text x="630" y="152" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">30</text>
  <text x="30" y="180" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">1</text>
  <text x="130" y="180" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-accent">2</text>
  <text x="230" y="180" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">1</text>
  <text x="330" y="180" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-accent">2</text>
  <text x="430" y="180" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">1</text>
  <text x="630" y="180" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono">0</text>
  <text x="20" y="208" class="t-sm t-accent">running count peaks at 2, so 2 rooms are enough</text>
</svg>

m14-gas-tank.caption: Gas Station with `diff = [-2,-2,-2,+3,+3]`: every negative tank resets the candidate start to the next station; from `start = 3` the tank stays non-negative, and `total = 0` confirms the loop is feasible.
m14-gas-tank.svg:
<svg viewBox="0 0 660 240" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Gas station running tank with resets and the chosen start">
  <text x="20" y="24" class="t-bold">gas station: diff = gas - cost, reset start when tank drops below 0</text>
  <rect x="20" y="44" width="44" height="44" class="f-bad-soft s-bad"/>
  <text x="42" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-bad">-2</text>
  <rect x="66" y="44" width="44" height="44" class="f-bad-soft s-bad"/>
  <text x="88" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-bad">-2</text>
  <rect x="112" y="44" width="44" height="44" class="f-bad-soft s-bad"/>
  <text x="134" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-bad">-2</text>
  <rect x="158" y="44" width="44" height="44" class="f-ok-soft s-ok"/>
  <text x="180" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-ok">+3</text>
  <rect x="204" y="44" width="44" height="44" class="f-ok-soft s-ok"/>
  <text x="226" y="72" text-anchor="middle" class="t-mono t-ok">+3</text>
  <text x="42" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">0</text>
  <text x="88" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">1</text>
  <text x="134" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">2</text>
  <text x="180" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">3</text>
  <text x="226" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-muted">4</text>
  <text x="42" y="140" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bad">reset</text>
  <text x="88" y="140" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bad">reset</text>
  <text x="134" y="140" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-bad">reset</text>
  <text x="180" y="140" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-ok">3</text>
  <text x="226" y="140" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-ok">6</text>
  <text x="290" y="140" class="t-sm t-muted">tank after each station (reset to 0 when negative)</text>
  <line x1="180" y1="200" x2="180" y2="160" class="s-accent" stroke-width="2" marker-end="url(#dg-arrow-accent)"/>
  <text x="180" y="226" text-anchor="middle" class="t-sm t-accent">start = 3 (one past the last failure); total = 0, so it is feasible</text>
</svg>
